incidents.victim: Multiple US enterprises (academic, aviation, retail, hospitality, automotive, financial services, legal, technology)
incidents.vector: Voice phishing (vishing) directed employees to adversary-in-the-middle SSO pages, capturing credentials and session tokens, then pivoting through identity providers into SaaS environments.
incidents.impact: Two new English-speaking extortion crews (CrowdStrike-tracked as Cordial Spider and Snarky Spider, aligned with Scattered Spider/The Com) have been running rapid data-theft and extortion campaigns since October 2025, with seven-figure ransom demands; non-payers have been hit with DDoS attacks and employee swatting.

incidents.victim: Moldova National Health Insurance Company (CNAM)
incidents.vector: Network intrusion into Moldova's compulsory health insurance database, with Moldovan cyber officials pointing to potential Russian involvement; no ransom was demanded.
incidents.impact: Possibly one-third of CNAM's database — personal information and healthcare payment records for a country of 2.4 million — was exfiltrated. Hospitals continue operating but data exposure scope is still under assessment.

incidents.victim: Brazilian regional ISPs
incidents.vector: Threat actor with root access to Brazilian DDoS-mitigation firm Huge Networks built a Mirai-variant botnet of TP-Link Archer AX21 routers (CVE-2023-1389) and abused unmanaged DNS resolvers for amplification attacks.
incidents.impact: Multi-year DDoS campaign hit small Brazilian ISPs in 10-60 second bursts using Huge Networks' own infrastructure and the CEO's leaked SSH keys. Huge Networks blames a January intrusion; the CEO claims a competitor framed the firm.

incidents.victim: France National Agency for Secure Documents (ANTS)
incidents.vector: Unauthorized access to ANTS' state identity-document platform; the suspect — a 15-year-old operating as 'breach3d' — extracted records and offered them on cybercriminal forums.
incidents.impact: 12-18 million records advertised for sale, including login credentials, names, emails, dates of birth, and account identifiers tied to passports, ID cards, and driver's licenses. Suspect placed under judicial supervision April 25.

incidents.victim: Delta Dental Insurance Company / Delta Dental of New York (NYDFS settlement)
incidents.vector: Threat actors exploited the 2023 MOVEit Transfer zero-day (CVE-2023-34362) to access Delta Dental's MOVEit servers and exfiltrate customer files.
incidents.impact: NYDFS announced a $2.25M penalty for inadequate incident response and late notification under 23 NYCRR Part 500. Compromised data included names, addresses, SSNs, driver's license numbers, financial account details, and patient health information.

incidents.victim: Spotswood Borough school district (New Jersey)
incidents.vector: Attackers compromised a school district employee's email account and impersonated the board of education to redirect a tax-funds transfer.
incidents.impact: $4.8 million in local school tax money was wired to attacker-controlled accounts. The borough is now issuing short-term notes to cover the gap while attempting to recover the funds.

incidents.victim: Sri Lanka Ministry of Finance
incidents.vector: Hackers breached the finance ministry's computer systems and diverted a foreign debt repayment.
incidents.impact: $2.5 million stolen during a debt repayment to Australia; a separate $600,000 payment owed to the US Postal Service is also missing. Sri Lanka has asked Australian law enforcement for help and is investigating broader financial-controls failures.

incidents.victim: Bundestag President Julia Klöckner / German political leadership
incidents.vector: Compromise of Klöckner's Signal account, part of a broader Russia campaign against Signal and WhatsApp accounts of European officials.
incidents.impact: Russian operators spied on a sensitive Signal group that included Chancellor Friedrich Merz, although Merz's own account was not compromised. Germany's cyber agency had previously warned of this targeting pattern.

incidents.victim: Kazakh-national defendant 'Sergei K.' / German defense industry targets
incidents.vector: HUMINT-cyber blend: suspect allegedly fed Russian intelligence details on German military aid to Ukraine, drone and robotics defense firms, and photographed public buildings and NATO military convoys; he also offered to recruit additional sources.
incidents.impact: Berlin arrest disclosed by federal prosecutors. Suspect was active for Russian intelligence in Germany since at least May 2025 and identified potential sabotage targets.

incidents.victim: Vimeo, Rockstar Games, McGraw Hill, ADT (Anodot supply-chain breach)
incidents.vector: Compromise of analytics vendor Anodot exposed authentication tokens that gave the ShinyHunters group access to cloud environments of more than a dozen Anodot customers without breaching the customers directly.
incidents.impact: Vimeo confirmed that user/customer technical data, video titles, metadata, and some email addresses were stolen via the Anodot breach; ShinyHunters added Vimeo to its leak site with a Thursday ransom deadline. Rockstar Games, McGraw Hill, and ADT have also been named.

incidents.victim: Cryptocurrency-sector executives (Web3, exchanges, VCs)
incidents.vector: BlueNoroff operators impersonate trusted contacts via Calendly/Telegram, swap Google Meet links for typo-squatted Zoom URLs, then run fake Zoom lobbies populated with stolen webcam footage, scraped photos, and AI-generated avatars; victims are then prompted to install a 'Zoom SDK update' (ClickFix) that drops malware.
incidents.impact: Arctic Wolf identified ~100 individuals (about half CEOs/co-founders) whose webcam footage was reused to lure new victims; full system compromise — credential theft, persistence, crypto-wallet and Telegram session theft — observed within five minutes of the initial click. One investigated US Web3 executive saw 66 days of attacker dwell time.

incidents.victim: Carnival Corporation customers
incidents.vector: Data theft attributed to ShinyHunters' broader 2026 campaign against major brands.
incidents.impact: Personal data of 7.5 million Carnival customers leaked online. Same campaign has hit Zara, 7-Eleven, Udemy, and Medtronic.

incidents.victim: Approximately 12,700 US organizations (HAFNIUM/Silk Typhoon historic compromise)
incidents.vector: Exploitation of Microsoft Exchange Server zero-days during 2020-2021 to drop webshells for persistent access, conducted by MSS contractor Shanghai Powerock Network.
incidents.impact: Italy extradited Chinese national Xu Zewei to the US, where he faces up to 62 years in prison for a campaign that compromised ~13,000 US organizations and stole COVID-19 vaccine research, law-firm data, and policymaker communications. Co-defendant Zhang Yu remains at large.

incidents.victim: OFAC sanctions action — Iran 'shadow banking' network
incidents.attacker: Iran — IRGC and state finance facilitators
incidents.vector: Iran's Bank Melli, Shahr Bank, and IRGC use 'rahbar' front-company networks (e.g., FSAQ, HMS Trading FZE) to launder proceeds from sanctioned oil sales and finance weapons procurement and proxy operations.
incidents.impact: Treasury designated 35 entities and individuals managing Iran's shadow banking architecture — adding to ~1,000 Iran-related designations since February 2025 — and warned that 'toll' payments to Iran for Strait of Hormuz passage trigger secondary-sanctions exposure.

incidents.victim: Tyler 'Tylerb' Buchanan (Scattered Spider) guilty plea
incidents.industry: Multi-sector (Twilio, LastPass, DoorDash, Mailchimp, Marks & Spencer victims)
incidents.vector: SMS-based phishing in 2022 that compromised at least a dozen tech companies, followed by SIM-swapping to drain individual cryptocurrency investors.
incidents.impact: 24-year-old Scottish national pleaded guilty to wire-fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft, admitting at least $8 million in cryptocurrency theft. Faces 20+ years; awaiting US sentencing.

incidents.victim: Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies (Cisco ASA/FTD devices)
incidents.vector: Exploitation of Cisco ASA firmware vulnerabilities CVE-2025-20333 and CVE-2025-20362 to deploy FIRESTARTER malware that survives firmware patching and provides persistent remote access.
incidents.impact: CISA and NCSC-UK released a joint malware analysis report and CISA updated Emergency Directive 25-03, requiring FCEB agencies to identify Firepower/Secure Firewall devices, collect forensics, and apply new vendor updates. CISA confirmed FIRESTARTER persisted on patched federal devices.

incidents.victim: Critical-infrastructure organizations targeted via SOHO router botnets
incidents.vector: China-nexus actors route operations through 'covert networks' — botnets of compromised SOHO routers and IoT/smart devices (e.g., Raptor Train, KV Botnet) provisioned by Chinese information-security companies such as Integrity Technology Group.
incidents.impact: NCSC, NSA, FBI, CISA, ASD/ACSC and 11 other allied agencies issued joint guidance describing how China-linked groups are using these networks to pre-position on critical infrastructure and conduct deniable espionage at scale, defeating static IOC-based defenses.

incidents.victim: Anthropic Claude Mythos preview
incidents.vector: Group of AI hobbyists allegedly used a contractor's leaked credentials and guessed Mythos operating endpoints to access the cybersecurity-agent service on launch day.
incidents.impact: Anthropic said the intruders 'played around' rather than running malicious prompts. Bloomberg reporting and subsequent EU finance-minister scrutiny of Mythos are amplifying concerns about the model's vulnerability-discovery capabilities.

incidents.victim: Ukrainian Prosecutor General's Office and allied militaries (Greece, Romania, Serbia)
incidents.vector: Targeted credential-phishing and email-account compromise against prosecutorial and military email systems.
incidents.impact: Email accounts of more than 170 Ukrainian prosecutors compromised in a campaign also targeting the militaries of three additional NATO/partner states, aimed at investigative case files.

incidents.victim: Hasbro Inc.
incidents.vector: March 2026 cyberattack against Hasbro corporate systems; details not disclosed.
incidents.impact: Hasbro delayed publishing Q1 financial results and reported continued shipping, invoicing, and finance-process disruption while restoring systems.

incidents.victim: Kelp DAO (DeFi platform)
incidents.vector: Smart-contract exploitation that allowed attackers to withdraw assets as rsETH (restaked Ether) tokens.
incidents.impact: $292 million in crypto assets drained — the largest crypto heist of 2026 to date. Crypto platforms have lost more than $606 million across 12 incidents in the past 18 days alone.

incidents.victim: Rockwell Automation OT/ICS deployments (US critical infrastructure)
incidents.vector: Cluster CL-STA-1128 (Cyber Av3ngers) deployed Rockwell FactoryTalk software on attacker-controlled VPS infrastructure to scale exploitation efforts against Allen-Bradley PLCs and FactoryTalk services exposed online.
incidents.impact: Cortex Xpanse identified ~5,600 IPs running Rockwell/Allen-Bradley SCADA services worldwide; CISA issued an April 7 advisory mirroring Unit 42's findings. Activity represents a shift from prior Unitronics PLC targeting and aligns with broader Iran retaliation following Operation Epic Fury.

incidents.victim: Vercel and downstream customers
incidents.vector: An infostealer infection at AI vendor Context.ai (disguised as a Roblox cheat) gave attackers access to Context.ai's Google Workspace; from there ShinyHunters pivoted to Vercel's cloud infrastructure credentials.
incidents.impact: Vercel disclosed the breach April 19 and warned a small number of customers to rotate keys and secrets. Stolen data is being auctioned online; downstream customer impact is still being assessed.

incidents.victim: Tallahassee, Florida (city government)
incidents.vector: Suspected ransomware incident; specifics undisclosed. Surrounding Leon County disconnected its IT network from the city to prevent contamination.
incidents.impact: Florida's capital city shut down its IT network on April 17; several city websites and public services remained unavailable for days. Behavior consistent with a ransomware containment response.
